tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - VMOVUPD: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
hint.title: Complex instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 16 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 144 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 112: addition or subtraction (16 inside FMA instructions)
 - 32: multiply (16 inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 1816 bytes (227 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 64 bytes (8 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.08 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 24.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 78% of peak load performance is reached (75.67 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 4% of peak store performance is reached (2.67 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header: 25% of peak computational performance is used (6.00 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.details:
97% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 94% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is highly vectorized.
98% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

gain.workaround: Reduce the number of FP add instructions
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by execution of FP add operations (the FP add unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 24.00 to 20.00 cycles (1.20x speedup).

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.20x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 16 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
